title: Remote sensing for healthy vegetation on green roofs
methods: temporal trend analysis by roof age

data_scope.imagery: very high-resolution multispectral orthoimagery (exact ground-sampling distance not stated in available metadata)

core_conclusion: Across 1,380 individual green roof units in Toronto (2011-2018), very high-resolution multispectral imagery shows green roofs become healthier and less patchy as they age. Vegetation health is driven mainly by unit area (positive), building height (negative) and vegetation type, with sedum mats generally healthier than woody plants and grasses. The study identifies critical thresholds in roof characteristics that sustain healthy vegetation, offering a transferable framework for long-term assessment and design optimization.
why_it_matters: Moves green-roof evaluation from single-site anecdote to a multi-year, city-wide remote-sensing census; shows green roofs improve over time; converts design intuition into evidence-based thresholds (area, height, plant type); ships open data (Zenodo) and code (GitHub) for reuse.
why_nature: Transferable analytical framework rather than a single case; links built-environment design to ecosystem services and urban well-being; strongly policy- and design-translatable thresholds; methodologically novel use of archival very-high-resolution multispectral imagery for fine-grained urban-vegetation monitoring.
added_value_to_my_research: Explainable-ML (SHAP/PDP) driver model of roof NDVI -> data-driven thresholds and interactions
Multi-city green-roof census -> channel for multi-source fusion and geospatial foundation-model fine-tuning
Couple vegetation health to cooling/runoff -> exposure half of an environment->health pipeline
Green-roof greenness as a spatial-equity and 2SFCA access-to-greenness exposure layer
Physics-informed spatiotemporal graph -> predict-then-prescribe roof design optimizer transferable to facility siting
